channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Explosions in Damascus Won’t Shake Macron’s Faith in Sharaa
item.description: France remains committed to bolstering cooperation with Syria.

item.title: The Myth of Chinese Birth Tourism
item.description: The United States has recast a small industry as a national security threat.

item.title: NATO Goes Shopping
item.description: Alliance members tout defense investments to try to head off Trump’s complaints.

item.title: Marine Le Pen is running for the French presidency
item.description: Despite being found guilty by the Paris court of appeal

item.title: AI has taken over the stock market. The bond market is next
item.description: Judging credit risk of the AI boom is difficult

item.title: Xi Is Defining History on His Own Terms
item.description: The language of the “new era” leaves real reform behind.

item.title: Europe’s economy is a mess. Its stock markets are a steal
item.description: International investors still aren’t interested

item.title: Democrats are in a mad rush to replace their Senate candidate in Maine
item.description: What Graham Platner’s collapse reveals about the party

item.title: What Graham Platner’s collapse means for the Democrats
item.description: The party is desperate to replace their Senate candidate in Maine

item.title: Taiwan Believes Japan Will Help Defend Against China
item.description: Opinion polling shows that trust in Tokyo is surprisingly deep.

item.title: How Trump Lost America the World Cup
item.description: A host is supposed to harvest the world’s goodwill, not burn it.

item.title: Rebuilding Syria will cost over $200bn. Bombs are not helping
item.description: Emmanuel Macron is greeted by explosions in Damascus

item.title: China’s semiconductor industry is racing to catch the West’s
item.description: But it is proving easier to design chips than to make them

item.title: Putin May Escalate, but Ukraine Is Winning
item.description: In the ongoing battle of narratives, Kyiv’s supporters have every reason to hold the course.

item.title: Iran Is Still Fighting a War Against Dissidents Abroad
item.description: Iranians in Europe say governments aren’t taking threats from Tehran seriously.

item.title: The Missing Ingredient for European Tech Sovereignty
item.description: Europe’s dependence on US technology companies is both an economic challenge and a strategic vulnerability. The European Union’s new Tech Sovereignty Package is a step in the right direction, but without equally ambitious efforts to curb market concentration, it is bound to fall short.

item.title: In Praise of the World Cup’s Little Guys
item.description: In a world of great-power rivalry and hyper-scaling tech behemoths, you would be forgiven for thinking that the bulky bully always has the edge. But as this year's World Cup has shown repeatedly, you would be wrong.

item.title: Not Just Rare Earths – Is This China’s Next Economic Weapon?
item.description: China’s dominance in the entire fluorine value chain is an overlooked source of geopolitical leverage.

item.title: Whither an International Response to China’s Ethnic Unity Law?
item.description: The conventional tools previously used to penalize China for its rights abuses – sanctions, public pressure, condemnation, institutional mechanisms, and more – have all been blunted.

item.title: China’s Submarine-launched Ballistic Missile Test Was a Message to the Pacific
item.description: The list of countries notified in advance, as well as the timing, suggests that China’s purpose was not merely a straightforward display of military power.

item.title: As the Dalai Lama Turns 91, India and China Are Fighting for the Future of Buddhism
item.description: With the revered Tibetan leader over 90 years old the rivalry between New Delhi and Beijing over who speaks for the Buddhist world is entering its most consequential phase.

item.title: From Partnership to Union: Australia and Fiji Sign 2 New Security Agreements
item.description: With the Vuvale Union and the Veitacini Treaty, Canberra and Suva chart a new course for regional security.

item.title: China’s Pacific SLBM Test Signals a New Phase in Undersea Nuclear Competition
item.description: The test, likely involving the JL-3 and coming just after the United States’ 250th Independence Day, underscored China's increasingly credible sea-based nuclear deterrent.

item.title: Inside Ukraine’s Game-Changing Drone Industry
item.description: In recent months, Ukrainian drones have successfully struck dozens of sites deep inside Russia, targeting the oil industry, weapons plants, and logistics hubs. But while Ukraine has developed a world-leading military technology to defend itself from a much larger aggressor, drones will not be enough to end the war.

item.title: Europe Is Struggling to Lead NATO
item.description: Germany and France are at the center of a clash over the alliance’s future.

item.title: Everyone Wants in on Brazil’s Rare Earths
item.description: But is Brasília ready to meet the moment?

item.title: Just Say No to Remote Work
item.description: Even during the immediate post-pandemic period, when evidence on the labor-market effects of remote work was scant, there was a strong case to be made for going into the office, especially for younger workers. And now, multiple new studies have vindicated this intuition.

item.title: Pakistan Ups the Ante on Indus Water Dispute With India
item.description: There is growing eagerness in Pakistan to involve China, the other upper riparian country, more directly in the issue.

item.title: Malaysia’s Upcoming State Elections: Allies Are Rivals Again
item.description: Despite being partners at the federal level, the Pakatan Harapan and Barisan Nasional coalitions are competing to become the dominant force of tomorrow.

item.title: Indonesia and Singapore Pledge That Malacca Strait Will Remain ‘Open to All’
item.description: The affirmations come after an Indonesian minister raised the idea of imposing a levy on vessels passing through the vital chokepoint.

item.title: How Europe Can Get Putin’s Attention
item.description: The continent must overcome its Russia predicament.

item.title: Build a Palestinian State
item.description: Save the two-state solution and stabilize the Middle East.

item.title: Can cutting-edge semiconductors supercharge Japan?
item.description: Visions of a high-tech hub in Hokkaido hinge on a single government-backed firm

item.title: The Rot Within: Thailand’s Interior Ministry In Focus
item.description: A series of recent scandals within the Ministry likely reflect tensions within the leadership of the ruling Bhumjaithai party.

item.title: Philippine Senate Opens Impeachment Trial of Vice President Sara Duterte
item.description: The 48-year-old faces charges of corruption, bribery, and an alleged plot to assassinate her rival, President Ferdinand Marcos Jr.

item.title: Philippine Vice President Sara Duterte Responds to Impeachment Allegations
item.description: The 48-year-old faces charges of corruption, bribery, and an alleged plot to assassinate her rival, President Ferdinand Marcos Jr.

item.title: China’s Closest Island to Taiwan Is a Monument to Reunification
item.description: Pingtan is marketed as mainland China's “closest point to Taiwan.” Today, the cross-strait ties that once made the island a thriving location for exchanges have all but disappeared.
